observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to enforce collection thereof to protect the interest of the government or request for authority from COA to write-off such long outstanding receivables through a Sanggunian Resolution, in line with COA Resolution No. 2003-002 dated January 30, 2003, stating the reasons for write-off and extent of follow-up made.
observations.managementAction: Of the long outstanding balance as of December 31, 2018 amounting to P1,025,076.28 , the Municipality collected P12,000.00 from Hon. Melania Alojado through salary deductions from January to December 2019.
observations.reason: The collection efficiency for the long outstanding accounts is very low.   Verification of the schedules as of December 31, 2019 submitted by the Municipal Accountant revealed that the dormant accounts with a long outstanding balance of P1,013,076.28 (net of the refund) still remained in the books under the Other Receivables account.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers in accordance with paragraph e, Section 4 of NGAS Manual, Volume I and Section 10 of the same Manual, Volume II.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant submitted monthly schedules of real accounts however, those were not supported with subsidiary ledgers.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to exert extra efforts to identify the breakdown of P254,194.30 balance from 2007.  Send demand letters to identified accountable officers and pursue the liquidation or settlement thereof through refund and/or withholding of salaries.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: The balance amounting to P254,194.30 pertaining to prior years’ cash advances remained unliquidated as at December 31, 2019.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor along with all the concerned officials and employees strictly follow, among others, Section 5.0 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 in the implementation of programs/projects for the 20% Development Fund.  See to it that those development projects identified in the Annual Investment Plan were fully implemented for the benefit of the constituents.  We further recommended that Municipal Mayor implement programs/ projects/
activities that are in line with social and economic development as well as environmental management concerns of the community in accordance with the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 of the DILG and DBM.  Stop the charging of expenses which do not constitute capital expenditures from the 20% Development Fund.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason:
Various projects sourced out from current appropriations were not implemented during the year.  Furthermore, there were two projects identified in the AIP which were not among the projects allowed to be charged against the Fund.

Reiterated in Observation No. 4 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipal treasurer provides complete and updated list of taxpayers with corresponding amount of real property tax due which shall be the basis of the municipal accountant in setting-up the actual RPT and SET receivables for the year pursuant to Section 20 of the NGAS Manual.
observations.managementAction: Preparations of updated list of taxpayers with corresponding amount of real property tax due and on encoding stage of PRIOR RPTAR’s. Program installed by province has not been working ever since.
observations.reason: Management is still awaiting for the completion of repairs made to the installed program.

observations.recommendation: The ICO-Municipal Treasurer should be required to restitute immediately the missing funds.
observations.managementAction: Ms. Suico has been terminated from the said government service. In spite of Ms. Suico’s promise to pay P1.4M upon sale of her Bogo lot, no payment has been received by the LGU.
observations.reason: No restitution of said funds has been reported to date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Municipal Accountant derecognize the barangay properties funded out of municipal funds in favor of the barangays. The Journal Entry Voucher (JEV)  dropping these barangay accounts from the municipal books shall be copy furnished the barangay as the basis of the Barangay Bookkeeper of recognizing the actual cost of PPE to be recorded in the barangay ledgers. In this way, the municipal books shall only contain those PPEs which are being actually used by the municipality and which are in their custody to properly attain the fair valuation of municipal PPE and corresponding municipal investment in the Government Equity.

We recommended that the Municipality should  always update the recording of the identified reconciling items in the Cash-in-Bank accounts which are derived from the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statement as verified by the Auditor in order to arrive at the accurate valuation of maintained depository accounts which shall be used by Management in rendering the appropriate decision on the use of municipal funds.

We recommended that Management should-
(1) be aware of reconciling items appearing in the Bank Reconciliation Statements such which affect the presentation of the fair balance of the depository accounts. Much more in this case, Management should have-
(2) noticed these reconciling items which had been dormant for years already and take action to address the situation. Management should-
(3) locate the payees of the stale checks especially the municipal employees and require them to file their request for check replacement afterwhich issue them their new checks to be acknowledged on a new disbursement voucher. In same manner, Management should-
(4) locate and properly identify the external creditors and issue them replacement checks to be acknowledged also on a new disbursement voucher. The non-presentation of the stale physical check in case it is inadvertently lost would not in any way harm the municipality because these could not be anymore negotiated for payment with the Bank and the creditor had already previously acknowledged payment when he issued the official receipt to the Municipality as attached to their disbursement vouchers while their acknowledgement on the new disbursement voucher would tantamount to their having received the new check payment after being properly identified as the legal and rightful claimant.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Abstract of Real Property Collections form at the end of each month in compliance with Sections 20 and 34 of the MNGAS and copy furnish the Provincial Accountant as prescribed for reconciliation purposes.

We recommended that Management enforce the liquidation of cash advances in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-004 and 97-002. Establish practicable mechanisms for the liquidation of all cash advances so that accumulation thereof in the books shall be prevented. It shall be the target of the Municipality to minimize the accumulation of balances by  the timely settlement through refund or the submission of liquidation documents within the prescribed period within which to liquidate them.

We recommended that the Municipality take serious action to recover the funds for disallowed transactions which were disbursed through illegal means considering the materiality of accounts involved.  Exert extra and strategic efforts to initiate collection of these collectibles to return back to the government the funds which were illegally disbursed. This is a serious matter and undertaking considering the huge amount of money at stake if this is lost due to Management negligence and inaction. All it takes is a firm resolution to attain this government purpose which is a constant yearly recommendation from COA recorded in the annual audit reports. Besides, the collection of disallowances is a law of the land, a duty which needs to be fulfilled by government officials and employees.

We recommended that the Municipality must be the catalyst for barangay development through the proper monitoring of the implementation of barangay projects according to their planned stages of completion in order to ensure that the benefit of economic progress shall address the needs of the people in the grassroots in a timely and efficient manner.

We recommended that Management –
Always require contractors to post their Performance Bond before the signing of the Contract with the Municipality;
Require the affected Contractors to post their Performance Bonds on unfinished projects as at year end; and
Always attach a copy of the Performance Security to the disbursement voucher as a reference to the faithful performance of the obligation of the Contractor.

We recommended that Management should require all affected Contractors to post additional Surety Bonds in the total amount of P5,657,160.19 for submission to the Municipality for their completed projects in order to protect the best interest of the Municipality of its completed infrastructure projects that is to obtain assurance that sufficient funds are available to finance the repairs of project structural defects that might occur before the final acceptance of the projects within one year after the projects were completed.

We recommended that Management –
Firmly assume responsibility over the recovery of the monies disbursed over these Receivables, putting into the right perspective that the Municipality is a corporate entity with going-concern and thus, its operations, issues and concerns should be addressed by incumbent personalities forming the Management. Management should appoint the group of persons who shall be accountable and responsible for  every stage of the collection process who shall exert their efforts and their dedication for this cause. Prepare a monthly monitoring report per borrower of the demand letters issued and received by them, number of visitations and the commitment of borrower per visitation and the amounts collected to show the progress of the implementation of the audit recommendation. The honest efforts to fully comply with these audit recommendations shall all ultimately redound to the benefit of the Municipality as additional funds to supplement future budgets or funds available to be appropriated for additional priority PPAs.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer reconcile their records on the Appropriations by the Budget Officer for the purchase and acquisition of PPE and the actual PPE expenditures based on the ledgers of the Municipal Accountant. In this way, the correct amount of Capital Outlay as budgeted and expended are reconciled and the disposition of funds for capital expenditures are properly accounted with.

We recommended that the Municipality shall always be aware of its outstanding Payables at the end of each fiscal year and obligate/charge them against the current year appropriations and correspondingly set up the Accounts Payable account in compliance with regulations. In this way, the recording of expenses in the year these were incurred is properly accounted  and the Expenses relating to the year shall all be taken up and the Government Equity as at the end of the year shall also be fairly valued. Management shall be aware of its responsibility for the proper disposition of government funds under their custody.

We recommended that to prevent the non-submission of required documents, the Accountant should attach a list of all required supporting documents per transaction to all vouchers presented in her office for processing, validation and certification for all claimants to comply with. In this way, requirements are submitted as listed to hasten the processing of claims and prevent delay in document review and processing when claims are not attended to because the validity of amounts due could not be established on insufficient attachments.

We recommended that Management particularly the Bids and Awards Committee (1) carefully prepare, (2) fully accomplish and (3) completely submit all necessary documents to bidding procurements for infrastructure projects for downloaded funds in order to support their validity, legality and propriety to avoid receiving Notice of Suspension requiring the submission of lacking documents or Notice of Disallowance for the return of monies for transacting illegal, invalid and irregular disbursements of government funds.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the General Fund as Income all collections from the PCSO for the municipal STL monthly shares.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer exert its efforts to recover copies of the market stalls lease agreements from the occupants. See to it that the documents are valid and devoid of defects so that Management can enforce fully the obligations of the stall holders in case of default in payment of dues, destruction of leased properties, violation of obligated duties and responsibilities and other instances which might harm or impair the rights and interests of the Municipal Government over the market and its productive operations.

We recommended that Management –
Identify properly the PPAs that needed implementation in the environment and business sectors, whether these are still applicable and need prioritization. If not, reprogram the funds to more pressing priority projects.
Fast track the implementation of these priority projects through proper project identification, planning of the implementation scheme and resource management such as equipment, personnel and materials considering the optimization of weather conditions which particularly affect the municipality.

We recommended that for the unimplemented Capital Outlay funds, Management should-
properly identify the PPAs that shall be accustomed to the actual needs of the times in order to properly respond to calamities and match the necessities that strengthen the capability of the LGU including the barangay level having the municipality as the core initiator, coordinator and implementer of calamity related PPAs of disaster risk reduction such as prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant requests the monthly updated status of loan accounts of borrower employees so that she is able to determine the sufficiency of amounts to be withheld from their salaries to satisfy the required monthly amortizations of their loans in order to help the borrower member avoid delinquency by withholding the proper amount for remittance to the GSIS. This action shall also assist the borrower maintain his good credit standing considering that information on their repayment performance on their GSIS loans are being provided to the Credit Information Corporation (CIC) as required by Republic Act No. 9510 also known as the Credit Information System Act, the repository of credit information from which credit checking and investigation could be accessed by all creditors whether public or private.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
reorganize the Inventory Committee through the issuance of an Executive Order;
require the constituted Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of all Inventories and PPE of the Municipality and submit the corresponding reports to the Audit Team; and
thereafter, instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the resulting balance of the physical count with the accounting records in order to present the correct balances of said accounts in the financial statements.
In the event that deficiencies/discrepancies exist between the Inventory Report and accounting records, the necessary adjustments/corrections should be made.  Also, require the Inventory Committee to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPEs in the Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) which could be undertaken simultaneous with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Development Council always ensure that the appropriations provided to support its development projects are sufficient as required by the existing laws, rules and regulations.
Moreover, we recommended that the MPDC and the Municipal Engineer:
strictly comply with Items 2.0 and 3.0 of the JMC in the identification of projects to be charged against the 20% DF;
carefully plan the implementation of the projects and effectively execute the construction schedule to be able to timely deliver the services to the intended beneficiaries thereof; and
submit additional photographs to justify the final payment made to the laborers relative to the project – Rehabilitation of Guintigbasan Multi-Purpose Hall.
We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer properly supervise the implementation of the projects which includes ensuring that the laborers who were hired for the actual construction thereof strictly follow the period of employment indicated in the respective Job Orders.
The Municipal Accountant is also recommended to thoroughly check the completeness of the supporting documents attached in the claims.
We likewise recommended that the MPDC, Municipal Engineer and Municipal Accountant jointly coordinate in the preparation of reports to be able to present complete and reliable information to the users thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant facilitate the submission of the aforementioned lacking documents to the Audit Team.  Moreover, we recommended that they should check the completeness and correctness of the supporting documents attached to the claims for payment of infrastructure projects in compliance with the provisions cited in COA Circular No. 2012-001.  If upon evaluation thereof, some of the information contained in some documents are inconsistent and/or the supporting documents are incomplete, require the concerned contractor to clarify the deficiencies noted and the submission of lacking documents before making payments thereto.
We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer, being the main overseer of the implementation of projects, should also ensure that the information to be presented in the documents/reports to be issued by the Municipality are consistent.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the:
Local Finance Committee identify additional sources of income;
Municipal Treasurer (i) determine doable measures to improve collection efficiency of internally-sourced taxes, fees and charges; (ii) keep a master list of stallholders and indicate therein the vital information such as stall/table number, description of location whether it is inside, outside or corner stall, stall area, rate per square meter or rate per day; and (iii) maintain a subsidiary ledger of each stallholder; and
Local Chief Executive execute a new contract of lease with the seven stallholders whose lease contracts has already expired.

We recommended that the persons liable or the Municipal Mayor in their behalf, file requests for installment payment to the PLO through the Audit Team on the remaining balance of the disallowance amounting to P305,192.05 in compliance with the aforementioned provisions.
Moreover, we recommended that they see to it that the requests are completely supported with appropriate documents such as the proposed installment schedule on a per ND basis.  In the preparation of the said schedule, consider the provisions of Item 5 of COA Resolution No. 2017-021.

We recommended that the management submit its legal basis on the
inclusion of lot acquisition under the build and design scheme for evaluation by
this office and to appropriately make manifestation with the National Housing
Authority on the noted deficiency in the aforementioned terms of reference.

We recommended that the management submit the Environmental
Clearance Certificate (ECC) pertinent to the above transaction for evaluation by
this office, otherwise a notice of disallowance will be issued to this effect.

Henceforth, we recommended that the management ensure that all
documentary requirements related to acquisition of real property are complied
with pursuant to the provisions of RA 8974 and COA Circular No. 2012-001
dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that the management submit justification on the noted
deficiency and to likewise review the amount and terms of the placement as well
as purpose of the funds for its proper disposition with the view of ensuring
efficiency, economy and effectiveness in its government operation.

We recommended that the management prioritize the implementation of
projects, programs and activities that are identified in the SWM plan and to
provide adequate funding to effectively implement relevant PPAs relative to
solid waste management.

We recommended that the management provide support to all barangays
in establishing MRF that is in accordance with this Act. We also recommended
that the municipality provide additional dump truck to accommodate the
collection services of the barangays.

We recommended that the management retrieve the supporting
documents of the dormant accounts payable and to examine and review such
documents. Further, communicate with the claimants for possible payment if
the transactions are valid and regular. For those with incomplete documents,
require the creditors to submit the documents as evidence of the claim, otherwise
the claim will be reverse to its appropriate account.

We recommended that the management require the contractor to
immediately undertake the necessary measures to rectify the deficiencies in the
project, otherwise, a refund of the equivalent amount should be collected from
the contractor.

We recommended that the municipal mayor and the sanggunian bayan
expedite the updating and amendment of the municipality’s local revenue code
in pursuance to Section 191 of R.A. 7160 to provide additional revenue sources
to fund the municipality’s projects and programs for the general welfare of its
constituents.

We recommended that the management submit its basis on the actual
rates used in the contract otherwise management consider amendments in the
contract or on the municipal revenue code as applicable and ensure that the
provisions and conditions are adequate in accordance with the prevailing local
government policies.

We recommended that the municipal treasurer prepare and submit report on
receivables to the municipal accountant as basis in the proper recording and
monitoring of the receivables.

We also recommended that the municipal accountant record and reflect
the necessary adjusting journal entry to recognize the amount of accumulated
uncollected fees and to present fairly the financial statements in accordance with
the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards.

The proposed adjusting journal entries to record the recognition of
receivables in the books is shown below:

Market Operations
Dr. Accounts Receivable 11,507.20
Cr. Prior Period Adjustment 11,507.20
Waterworks Operations
Dr. Accounts Receivable 155,202.00
Cr. Prior Period Adjustment 155,202.00
Maricaban Land rental
Dr. Accounts Receivable 261,342.40
Cr. Prior Period Adjustment 261,342.40

If there is an objective evidence that part of the accounts receivables are
impaired, the entry below is suggested:
Dr. Impairment Loss – Loans and Receivables xxxx
Cr. Allowance for Impairment-Accounts Receivable x x xx

We recommended that the management collect from the market stall
occupants the corresponding deposits and advance payment otherwise impose
sanctions for non-compliance thereof.

We recommended that the management submit a justification on the
noted deficiencies and execute the required MOA outlining the standards of the
project’s implementation and subsequently monitor compliance thereof in
accordance with such standards pursuant to the rules and procedures set forth
in COA Circular No. 2007-001.

We recommended the management to secure a valid official receipt from
the League of Municipalities in accordance with the National Internal Revenue
Code and BIR regulations. Furthermore, we also recommended that the
management submit justification and legal basis in providing financial assistance
to the League of Municipalities.

We recommended that the concerned chief executive direct the municipal
accountant to submit immediately the subject records, disbursement vouchers,
supporting documents and other reports due for submission to avoid
disallowance in audit.

We recommended that Management attach all relevant supporting documents to transactions to enable the Auditor to render the proper result of evaluation whether funds were or were not disbursed legally and properly. Submission of the required documents gives assurance that the Municipality is paying the commensurate amount of project cost on the actual stage of project accomplished. Likewise, COA issues either Notice of Suspension or Notice of Disallowance as a result of non-submission of documentary requirements as prescribed in COA Circular 2012-001 and Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Management has fully documented claims/payroll  of laborers

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality establish PPAs that are adaptable to the physical conditions of the community on disaster preparation, prevention and mitigation and rehabilitation and recovery to ensure the safety and sustained socio-economic welfare of its constituents.
observations.managementAction: Applicable PPAs were adopted by reprogramming previous years’ LDRRM funds.

observations.recommendation: We congratulate the Management for a job well done. We also recommended, in addition,  that the Municipality exert more efforts to disseminate information on their program for waste disposal hoping to maintain the Zero Waste status quo which shall be affecting favorably the promotion of sanitation and health in the community.
observations.managementAction: Information dissemination was implemented through jingles announced within the municipal roads and also reached out to the barangays through the Punong Barangays and councils.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accountant prioritize the reconciliation of the Due to National Government subsidiary ledgers with the Trial Balance as at year-end to establish proper ending balances to be forwarded in the ensuing year. Moreover, Management shall obtain the proper information and continue implementing the projects in accordance with funds still available.
observations.managementAction: The Accountant is still in the process of reconciling Trust Liabilities due to the national government agencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that as at the end of the calendar year, the Accountant should return back to the Cash-in-Bank balance the total amount of unreleased checks to reflect the accurate and proper amount of depository accounts in the financial statements. The Treasurer should give the list of unreleased checks to the Accountant for proper recording.
observations.managementAction: The bank balances were overstated due to untreated stale checks still outstanding in the books. Unreleased checks were minimized and payments due January 2020 for December 2019 payables were prepared and paid in January 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact his collections in the General Fund as at year-end especially on the last transaction day of every fiscal year in order to safekeep municipal funds at the depository bank and prevent the possibility of loss or misuse of scarce resources. On the stale checks, the Accountant should adjust the amount to Cash-in-Bank instead of to Cash-in-Local Treasury to return back the deducted amount of checks issued but should be returned back to the bank balance because of their being estopped from being transacted.
observations.managementAction: Undeposited collection balance as at year end was maintained at minimal level.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accountant always attach adequate if not complete supporting schedules to real accounts in the year-end FS to fully explain what these accounts are made of in compliance with government reporting requirements in Section 111 of PD 1445.
observations.managementAction: The FS at year end still lack necessary schedules.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that for the ensuing year, Management shall be vigilant and put up a plan of procurement so that the unimplemented projects as at the end of the year shall be purchased/constructed in 2019 in order that the people enjoy the benefits of protection in preparation of and from the aftermaths of disasters.
observations.managementAction: Several PPAs were unimplemented while others were fully implemented and the remainder of the project funds were reprogrammed to other LDRRMF priority purposes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that as at the end of the year, all expenses of the Municipality shall be booked and recognized even the petty operating expenses spent from the Petty Cash Fund in order to present properly the correct amounts of Expense accounts and the resulting Government Equity which is decreased in case of net loss and increased in case of net income from operations.
observations.managementAction: Minimal balance of PCF in an amount lesser than last FY2018 was not returned as at year end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management enforce the liquidation of cash advances in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 as stated above.
observations.managementAction: Reduction of 27% in amount was made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accountant prioritize the reconciliation of the Due to LGUs subsidiary ledgers with the Trial Balance as at year-end to establish proper ending balances to be forwarded in the ensuing year. Moreover, Management shall obtain the proper information and continue implementing the projects in accordance with funds still available. The barangay with negative balances shall earn in their accounts amount that offset the overdraft and return the funds to the proper claimants.
observations.managementAction: Still in reconciliation process.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality take serious action to recover the funds for disallowed transactions through legal means if necessary considering the materiality of accounts involved and the corresponding costs of collecting them. Exert extra and strategic efforts to initiate collection of these collectibles to return back to the government the funds which were illegally disbursed.
observations.managementAction: Significant decrease in collectibles was made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer remit in full taxes withheld within a month in the ensuing month. Coordinate with the Accountant on the details of accounts to be paid. Ensure the equality of amount of tax withheld and remitted to give the government the needed funds for its continuous operations.
observations.managementAction: Taxes withheld in December were remitted in January the following year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality provide legal basis of granting the CREA otherwise, same shall be returned back to the government coffers for having been disbursed in violation of government laws, rules and regulations.  The Municipality is also advised to refrain from duplicating the granting of benefit of same nature in future operations.
observations.managementAction: No disbursement of irregular additional benefits during the audit year.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation last year that Management should base their PS cap according to Section 325(a) of the Local Government Code in the preparation of the annual budget from which items of PS shall be planned. Stop the practice of appropriating more than what the law for PS limitation requires. Excess expenditures over the PS cap shall be subject for disallowance in conformity with the provisions of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC and the TWG submit full supporting documents to substantiate their claim for honoraria from municipal procurements for the Auditor to render a favorable decision to pass the transaction in audit, otherwise, absence of requirements would force her to disallow them in audit for not conforming with government laws, rules and regulations particularly COA Circular No. 2012-001 and DBM Circular No. 2004-5A.
observations.managementAction: BAC and TWG honoraria were disbursed properly.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated that Sections 63 and 68 of PD No. 1445 be implemented to establish proper accountability for collections which could be made anytime whenever audit procedures require. The practice therefore of issuing official receipts immediately for all collections shall be observed. Regarding the collections at the Balete Pass, cash tickets in denominations of P5, P10 and P20 which may be acquired from the National Printing Office could be utilized instead of Accountable Form 51 which  may be issued for greater amounts such as rentals of picnic huts, nipa huts, cottages and the halls. All receipts including unused cash tickets for the day should be accounted with the beginning cash tickets held and the received cash tickets for use to arrive at the total collections which may be remitted daily or as agreed upon for convenience of both the employee and the Municipal Treasurer considering that there are also lean days during the week.

On the sales of the OTOP Store, a regular inventory taking of twice a month may be observed to report items sold from beginning inventory and purchases after an ending inventory is established from physical count. A certain profit percentage on cost should be established per product classification should also be observed to facilitate the computation of sales. Inventory items should be grouped according to nature to facilitate inventory taking and reporting. The Accountable Form No.51 should be issued for the sales at the OTOP Store which should be remitted regularly which can be done once a day. Inventory items shall be encoded in forms which shall be used to account for the quantity at the beginning, quantity received, quantity of inventory end to arrive at the quantity sold.

We also observed that sales of the OTOP store were recorded as  a trust receipt which was rolled over for purchases. Since it is an enterprise in the LGU with grants already received for its proper establishment, the sales of the OTOP Store should be properly accounted as income generated in the General Fund which means that if income increase, then the basis for the limitation of Personnel Services also increase, thus is beneficial for the increase in salaries of the municipal officials and employees 

With a new way of handling and managing cash in the economic enterprise, the Municipality is assured of performing better commensurate with the services they render to the people. It may be hard at the start but once established, proper procedures and good practices shall be learned in due time.
observations.managementAction: Management still has some improvements to make with their collection and disbursement systems for the MTPDO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor strictly observe the general limitations on personal services cited under Section 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and LBC No. 98.  Also, she should always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements are completely complied with.    As part of her fiscal responsibility, she should allow only payments of personal services costs and other expenditures expressly authorized by law.
observations.managementAction: The Management has already complied with the rules on PS limitation.    For CY 2019, the actual PS cost of the Municipality for CY 2019 did not exceed the PS limitation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure strict compliance with Section 96 (a) and (b) of R.A. No. 7160 everytime she will travel outside the province.  

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly check the claims submitted to the Accounting Office in order to arrive at the amount of travelling expenses which the itinerant is entitled to.
observations.managementAction: Verification of the disbursement vouchers from May to December 2019 pertaining to the travels of the Municipal Mayor disclosed that these were already supported with Travel Orders approved by the Provincial Governor.  In the absence of Travel Orders, letters addressed to the Provincial Governor requesting for the issuance of the said document duly received by the Office of the latter were attached instead.  Likewise, the specific purposes of travels were already indicated in the Certificates of Appearance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant facilitate the submission of the aforementioned lacking documents to the Audit Team.  Moreover, we recommended that they should check the completeness and correctness of the supporting documents attached to the claims for payment of infrastructure projects in compliance with the provisions cited in COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer make representations to the officers of TIELCO for the installation of the secondary line in order for the water system to become operational.
observations.managementAction: The photographs attached in the claims of the contractor were already labeled.  Furthermore, the dates of the documents submitted on some payments were consistent.   The Municipal Engineer also conducted follow-ups with the officers of the TIELCO regarding the installation of the secondary line in order for the water system to become operational.
observations.reason:
Various lapses were still noted in the audit of payments of infrastructure projects for CY 2019.

At present, the water system in Barangay Poblacion was already operational upon inspection conducted on February 19, 2020.  Interview with the Municipal Engineer disclosed that the water system situated in Barangay Guinbirayan is also operational as of this reporting.  On the other hand, the officers of the TIELCO has already installed the secondary line awaiting for the installation of the transformer on the water system located in Barangay Magsaysay to become operational.

observations.recommendation:
The Audit Team also verified the compliance of the BAC with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in the procurement activities undertaken for the projects charged against the 20% DF and observed that: 

PROCUREMENT 

The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) committed procedural lapses in the procurement activities undertaken for the projects charged against the 20% DF which were not in accordance with the provisions of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act (R.A.) No. 9184, thus affecting the propriety of the Purchase Orders (POs).
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant already prepared and submitted the List of Vouchers/
Projects Charged Against the 20% DF pertaining to the continuing projects.  The Management has also significantly reduced the projects which do not constitute the nature of investment or capital expenditures.
observations.reason:
Based on the available documents, various projects sourced out from the current appropriations were not implemented during the year.   Furthermore, there were continuing projects which still remained unimplemented as of December 31, 2019.   Verification of the AIP disclosed that there were two projects which were classified under MOOE hence, majority of the disbursements were recorded in MOOE accounts.  Two projects identified in the AIP were also not among the projects allowed to be charged against the Fund.   The Audit Team likewise noted incomplete and inconsistent information presented in the related reports on some projects.    

Reiterated in Observation No. 4 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor strictly observe the general limitations on personal services cited under Section 325 (a) of R.A. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to remit to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth the withheld contributions and loan amortizations intact and within the prescribed period in compliance with R.A. No. 8291, R.A. No. 9679 and R.A. No. 7875 to avoid incurrence of penalties. Since CY 2018, the Municipal Accountant already observed the proper recording of the withholding and remittance of contributions and premiums.

instruct the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant to include in their reports the continuing projects of the Municipality to be able to provide complete and accurate information to the readers thereof.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management stop the practice of recognizing fund transfers received for specific purpose as regular income to avoid incurring excess of PS cost over PS limitation.

We further recommended that Management strictly observe the general limitations on personal services cited under Section 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98 dated October 14, 2011.  The Municipal Mayor, being the Local Chief Executive, should always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements are completely complied with.  As part of her fiscal responsibility, she should allow payments of personal services costs and other expenditures only expressly authorized by law.
observations.managementAction: The Management has already complied with the rules on PS limitation.   For CY 2019, the actual PS cost of the Municipality for CY 2019 did not exceed the PS limitation.    Also, the Management already stopped the practice of recognizing fund transfers received for specific purpose as regular income.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exert effort in determining if the National Solid Waste Management Commission approved their 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan.   Moreover, comply strictly with the provisions of Republic Act No. 9003.

During the year, the Municipality has procured 1 unit plastic shredder machine and 1 set brick moulder (steel made) totalling P296,000.00 to be used in the solid waste management.  This is sourced out from the 20% DF for CY 2018.
observations.reason: The 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan (2013-2022) submitted by the Municipality was conditionally approved by the NSWMC.  Also, the target date of the Municipality towards the establishment of sanitary landfill which is required by R.A. No. 9003 is by year 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management promote and implement a comprehensive Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan that supports disaster risk management activities. Reiterated in Observation No. 2 of the report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:
The Municipal Treasurer:

Instruct all collectors to prepare RCDs and remit all collections to the Municipal Treasurer in accordance with Section 29, Vol. 1 of the NGAS Manual;

Be more circumspect in the acknow-ledgment of remittances in terms of accuracy of the amount;

Refrain from allowing incomplete remittances from the collecting officers.

The Municipal Accountant:

Ensure the accuracy of the amounts of recorded transactions and adjust the balance of the accounts affected by the miscal-culated collections; and 

The practice of charging the unremitted collections to receivable account be refrained as this only encourages delayed remittance and/or incomplete remittance or non-remittance at all of the collections receipted by the collecting officers.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had already stopped the practice of recording unremitted collections by debiting the Due from Officers and Employees account.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant was advised to give priority to the timely submission of the accounts and financial reports within the reglementary period as required under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009, COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995 and Section 70, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs to enable the Audit Team to conduct timely audit thereof and promptly inform the Management of the deficiencies noted, if any.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management speed up the implementation of said projects and programs funded under the Bottom-Up Budgeting (BuB) and ensure that funds are optimally utilized with a view of ensuring efficiency, economy and effectiveness in the utilization of government resources for the benefit of its constituencies. Otherwise, the management shall return such fund to the source agency so that appropriate agencies could utilize the same for other equally important government projects.

observations.recommendation:

We further recommended that the BAC and TWG exercise utmost prudence in the post-qualification of bids in order to properly determine whether the bidder concerned complies with and are responsive to all the requirements and conditions specified in accordance with the R.A. 9184 and its IRR and the GPPB Circular, otherwise, persons responsible should be held accountable.

Furthermore, any review comments by the provincial finance committee on municipal budgets that needs to be complied with strictly must be taken into consideration before disbursing funds to avoid disallowance in post-audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management speed up the implementation of said projects and programs funded under the Bottom-Up Budgeting (BuB) and ensure that funds are optimally utilized with a view of ensuring efficiency, economy and effectiveness in the utilization of government resources for the benefit of its constituencies. Otherwise, the management should return such fund to the source agency so that appropriate agencies could utilize the same for other equally important government projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the OIC-Accountant reconcile the general ledger figures with the distributed amounts in the subsidiary ledgers by (1) tracing entries starting at the time they could not any more prepare subsidiary details due to accounts fragmentation, (2) distributing the lump sum amounts lodged in a separate subsidiary ledger, and, (3) adjusting the affected accounts for errors made, in order to come up with faithful reporting of liability accounts that could improve the fairness with which the financial statements are prepared.
observations.managementAction: Management has not yet complied with the audit recommendation
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 11 page 56 of this Report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management (1) trace the movement of the RPT and SET Receivables and corresponding Deferred Credits, (2) segregate from the Report of Monthly Collections the 25% for the Barangay, 35% for the Province and 40% for the Municipality on RPT-Basic, (3) segregate from the same Report of Monthly Collections the 50% for the Province and the 50% for the Municipality on SET, (4) prepare JEV to adjust the affected accounts based on the results of segregating the amounts of shares of the Barangays, Province and Municipality, (5) prepare the appropriate JEV entry following the pro-forma entry for tax collections in 2018 and in the succeeding periods in order to come up with the true balance of collectibles from taxpayers as at December 31, 2018.
observations.managementAction: The same audit finding is reiterated in this AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that (1) the Municipal Treasurer record in the Report of Checks Issued only those checks which were already released and acknowledged by payees during the period; (2) the Municipal Accountant record in the Check Disbursements Journal only those checks verified to be issued to the payees as evidenced by their duly dated acknowledgement receipt on the last box of the disbursement voucher; stop the practice of portraying that liabilities were already settled as at the end of the period when actual payment was not yet delivered, in order to reflect the true balance of the depository account with the AGDB.
observations.managementAction: Unreleased checks were also not returned back to the depository account. This is one of the findings in this AAR.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 9 page 52 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accounting Office (1) trace back whether the transactions of prior years were recorded in reciprocity by preparing subsidiary ledgers per fund per account, (2) prepare working paper showing the discrepancies in recording the affected accounts, (3) adjust the accounts based on the cumulative effects to accounts as concluded in the working papers, and (4) submit copy of the WP to COA as part of the agency compliance to AAR recommendations  to be reported in the AAPSI.
observations.managementAction: There still exists a discrepancy between these two accounts as reported in this AAR.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 17 page 67 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Finance Committee whose major role and function is to manage the sources and uses of municipal funds should abide by the provisions of the Local Government Code, in planning the appropriation and disbursement of municipal moneys, in order that the municipality can implement its target, plans and projects through the maximum utilization and application of municipal funds to provide quality services to its officials, employees and the public as a whole. Return to the municipal coffers the P1,881,712.50 cost of excess Personnel Benefits to be issued with a Notice of Disallowance  to enforce settlement thereof.
observations.managementAction: Management incurred overdraft in PS limit.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 22 page 80 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (1) report and account all issued accountable forms, (2) refrain from maintaining cash reserves in the municipal coffers,  (3) deposit in full and intact all collections from the issuance of accountable forms, (4) the collectors remit daily and in full all collections of the Municipality, and (5) the Municipal Treasurer establish through the issuance of memorandum to all collectors the policies and guidelines in reporting and managing their collections such that a cut-off time should be observed daily to delineate the period when collections are reported and accounted in the municipal coffers which should be convenient for the Liquidating Officer to deposit them daily and intact with the authorized government depository bank in accordance with the pertinent provisions of the Revised Cash Examination Manual in order to safeguard government funds from the risk of possible loss.
observations.managementAction: Same finding is attached to this AAR on this subject.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality implement the planned programs and activities for the prevention and mitigation of disasters,  in order that it ensures its readiness to manage the risks brought about by future disasters and is able to protect well the welfare of its constituents.
observations.managementAction: Same finding exists in this audit year on the same subject.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 12 page 58 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) require the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of all Inventories and Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) and submit to the Audit Team the RPCI and RPCPPE; and b) thereafter, instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the resulting balance of the physical count with the accounting records in order to present the correct balances of said accounts in the financial statements. In the event that deficiencies/discrepancies exist between the Inventory Report and accounting records, the necessary adjustments/corrections should be made. Also, require the Inventory Committee to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPEs in the Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) which could be undertaken simultaneously with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee of the Municipality was reorganized, thus, physical count of the properties were not conducted at year-end. Consequently, the preparation of the corresponding inventory reports was not made. Reiterated in Finding No. 1 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor strictly observe the general limitations on personal services cited under Section 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98 dated October 14, 2011. Also, she should always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements are completely complied with. As part of her fiscal responsibility, she should allow only payments of personal services costs and other expenditures expressly authorized by law.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: For CY 2018, the Municipality still exceeded the PS limitation by P5,613,418.41 per budget and P3,431,480.90 per actual expenditure. This is more than the resulting PS excess computed in CY 2017. Reiterated in Finding No. 2 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a) Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council (LDRRMC) to comply strictly with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002; b) Municipal Accountant to adhere strictly to the accounting and reporting guidelines set forth under COA Circular No. 2012-002; and c) LDRRMO to submit the duly accomplished templates on the Status of DRRMF as of December 31, 2017.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: The LDDRMO still failed to submit the duly accomplished templates on the Status of DRRMF as of December 31, 2017 to the Audit Team. Also, other observations discussed in the previous year were still addressed in CY 2018 audit. Reiterated in Finding No. 3 of this report. The BAC implemented some of the audit recommendations; however, some procurement activities for goods in CY 2018 still disclosed lapses in the procurement undertaken by the BAC.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor: a) require the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of all Inventories and Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) accounts of the Municipality so that the corresponding report can be submitted to the Audit Team; and b) thereafter, instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the resulting balance of the physical count with the accounting records in order to present the correct balances of said accounts in the financial statements. In the event that deficiencies/discrepancies exist between the Inventory Report and accounting records, the necessary adjustments/corrections should be made. Also, require the Head of the Inventory Committee to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPEs in the IIRUP which could be undertaken simultaneous with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: The Management failed to conduct physical count of the properties at year-end. Reiterated in Finding No. 1 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to exert extra efforts to identify the breakdown of the prior years’ cash advances balance from 2007. Send demand letters to identified accountable officers and pursue the liquidation or settlement thereof through refund and/or withholding of salaries.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: The balance amounting to P254,194.30 pertaining to prior years’ cash advances remained unliquidated as of December 31, 2018.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor: a) require the Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of all Inventories and PPE and submit the corresponding report to the Audit Team; and b) thereafter, instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the resulting balance of the physical count with the accounting records in order to present the correct balances of said accounts in the financial statements. In the event that deficiencies/discrepancies exist between the Inventory Report and accounting records, the necessary adjustments/corrections should be made. Also, require the Municipal Treasurer to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPEs in the IIRUP which could be undertaken simultaneous with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to enforce collection thereof or request for authority from COA to write-off such long outstanding receivables through a refund Sanggunian Resolution in line with COA Resolution No. 2003-002 dated January 30, 2003, stating the reasons for write-off and extent of follow-up made.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.

Verification of the schedules as collected revealed that the dormant accounts with a long outstanding balance of P1,025,076.28 (net of the refund) still remained in the books under Other Receivables account.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to maintain the required subsidiary ledgers for all funds as prescribed by NGAS Manual, Volume I and II.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant submitted monthly schedules of real accounts, however those schedules were not supported with subsidiary ledgers.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Accountant was advised to give priority to the timely submission of the accounts and financial reports within the reglementary period as required under COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009, COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995 and Section 70, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs to enable the Audit Team to conduct timely audit thereof and promptly inform the Management of the deficiencies noted, if any.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: Analysis of the submission of the accounts and trial balances showed that delay was still incurred by the Accounting Office.

Send demand letters to identified accountable officers and pursue the liquidation or settlement thereof through refund and/or withholding of salaries.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: The balance amounting to P254,194.30 pertaining to prior years’ cash advances remained unliquidated as of December 31, 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor along with all concerned officials and employees follow Section 5.0 of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 in the implementation of programs/projects for the 20% Development Fund and see to it that those development projects identified in the Annual Investment Plan were fully implemented for the benefit of the constituents.
