observations.recommendation:
1.7	The municipality is recommended to charge to the 20% Development Fund only those expenses which were disbursed for capital expenditures and contribute to the creation of tangible physical existence of the investments allowed in the Fund. Charging against the Fund of wages of laborers and contractual personnel not contributing to the creation/addition to capital assets/investments is discouraged. Consider adding to the capital asset accounts those investment expenditures which are aligned to the Fund purposes. Rectify and adjust the affected asset accounts to reflect their correct values/costs in the succeeding financial statements of the Municipality.
1.8	On the cost of labor which are spent for the fabrication/production of capital assets for the Forest Park economic enterprises and WATSAN, we recommended that separate program of works to show the estimates for materials and labor components is needed from which to base all capital charges. Such program of works shall be attached to all the corresponding vouchers to signal that these claims need to be capitalized under the 20 DF%.
1.9	The Municipality is also encouraged to separate from the 20% DF all non-capital outlay components and charge them from the corresponding office appropriations in the General Fund.
1.10	We also recommended that Management return to the 20%DF the misused monies and reprogram them for other priority development projects as identified by the Local Development Council. The status of implementation of these recommendations shall be reported and monitored in succeeding audit periods.

2.9	We also recommended that the LGU augment their pool of heavy equipment to address the need for readiness at all times in order to simultaneously implement projects and maximize manpower and utilization of equipment during good weather. Procurements through public bidding must be properly planned and conducted simultaneously to give opportunity to various suppliers and contractors to participate in the provision of materials and services for municipal projects. Augment also the personnel in the Engineering Office to attend to the preparation of program/project estimates and expedite the preparation of procurement documents. Additional personnel should also be hired to monitor project implementation to ensure that projects are implemented according to specifications to attain their desired and optimum quality.
2.8	It was also observed that some minimal amounts of residues on fully implemented projects still remain in the Fund which we recommend to be reprogrammed to fund other priority development projects.

3.6	We recommended that Management attach all relevant supporting documents to transactions to enable the Auditor to render the proper result of evaluation whether funds were or were not disbursed legally and properly. Submission of the required documents gives assurance that the Municipality is paying the commensurate amount of project cost on the actual stage of project accomplished. Likewise, COA issues either Notice of Suspension or Notice of Disallowance as a result of non-submission of documentary requirements as prescribed in COA Circular 2012-001 and Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.

4.17	We recommended that the Municipality establish PPAs that are adaptable to the physical conditions of the community on disaster preparation, prevention and mitigation and rehabilitation and recovery to ensure the safety and sustained socio-economic welfare of its constituents before, during and after disasters.

5.6	In general, the Municipality has addressed gender-responsive priorities to obtain equality of sexes based on the sex-disaggregated data which were taken through the Community Based Monitoring System CBMS) which facilitated their identification of GAD priority programs. Majority, although short of some minimal gender issues pertaining to women and children were properly addressed through the GAD Focal Point System and we recommended that the privileges of men shall also be given priority and allocation of funds in succeeding annual GAD plans in compliance with the issued JMCs.

6.9	We congratulate the Management for a job well done. We also recommended, in addition,  that the Municipality exert more efforts to disseminate information on their program for waste disposal hoping to maintain the Zero Waste status quo which shall be affecting favorably the promotion of sanitation and health in the community.

7.5	We recommended that the Accountant prioritize the reconciliation of the Due to National Government subsidiary ledgers with the Trial Balance as at year-end to establish proper ending balances to be forwarded in the ensuing year. Moreover, Management shall obtain the proper information and continue implementing the projects in accordance with funds still available.

8.5	We recommended that Management prepare and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment by the designated Property Inventory Committee by indicating also therein the cost and the persons in charge and their respective offices to pinpoint accountability;  reconcile it with the accounting records;  book-up donated PPE in accordance to the Memorandum of Agreement or Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment, as the case may be which was executed  by the donor agency;  segregate unserviceable items beyond repair and report them in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) so that the PPE accounts are properly accounted in the financial statements and unserviceable items are disposed within the limits of authority; and identify lost items and request for relief from accountability.

9.3	We recommended that as at the end of the calendar year, the Accountant should return back to the Cash-in-Bank balance the total amount of unreleased checks to reflect the accurate and proper amount of depository accounts in the financial statements. The Treasurer should give the list of unreleased checks to the Accountant for proper recording.

10.10	We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact his collections in the General Fund as at year-end especially on the last transaction day of every fiscal year in order to safekeep municipal funds at the depository bank and prevent the possibility of loss or misuse of scarce resources. On the stale checks, the Accountant should adjust the amount to Cash-in-Bank instead of to Cash-in-Local Treasury to return back the deducted amount of checks issued but should be returned back to the bank balance because of their being estopped from being transacted.

11.7	We recommended that the Accountant always attach adequate if not complete supporting schedules to real accounts in the year-end FS to fully explain what these accounts are made of in compliance with government reporting requirements in Section 111 of PD 1445.

12.5	We recommended that for the ensuing year, Management shall be vigilant and put up a plan of procurement so that the unimplemented projects as at the end of the year shall be purchased/constructed in 2019 in order that the people enjoy the benefits of protection in preparation of and from the aftermaths of disasters.

13.3	We recommended that as at the end of the year, all expenses of the Municipality shall be booked and recognized even the petty operating expenses spent from the Petty Cash Fund in order to present properly the correct amounts of Expense accounts and the resulting Government Equity which is decreased in case of net loss and increased in case of net income from operations.

We recommended that Management send demand letters to these persons asking for the return of the amount due from them. Send two succeeding tracers to reinforce the intention of the Municipality to recover the funds. Strategize collection through the joint efforts of concerned municipal offices to properly return back government monies to the municipal coffers.  Otherwise, the balances shall be disallowed for lack of documents that shall warrant their liquidation considering the long period of time the funds had stayed out of government coffers and those who authorized their payments shall be solidarily liable with the payee-recipients.
15.10	We recommended that Management enforce the liquidation of cash advances in compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 as stated above.

16.5	We recommended that the Accountant prioritize the reconciliation of the Due to LGUs subsidiary ledgers with the Trial Balance as at year-end to establish proper ending balances to be forwarded in the ensuing year. Moreover, Management shall obtain the proper information and continue implementing the projects in accordance with funds still available. The barangay with negative balances shall earn in their accounts amount that offset the overdraft and return the funds to the proper claimants.

17.5	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile these two accounts by going back to the previous transactions, analyzing what should be made and preparing the JEV to adjust the accounts to reflect the proper real account balances.

18.7	We recommended that the Municipality take serious action to recover the funds for disallowed transactions through legal means if necessary considering the materiality of accounts involved and the corresponding costs of collecting them. Exert extra and strategic efforts to initiate collection of these collectibles to return back to the government the funds which were illegally disbursed.

19.9	We recommended that the Accountant reconcile the RPT and SET Receivables and related Deferred Tax accounts per year to facilitate comparison of data; Adjust the affected accounts whenever necessary in order to depict the true value of collectible real property taxes as at year end.

20.5	We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer remit in full taxes withheld within a month in the ensuing month. Coordinate with the Accountant on the details of accounts to be paid. Ensure the equality of amount of tax withheld and remitted to give the government the needed funds for its continuous operations.

21.8	We recommended that the Municipality provide legal basis of granting the CREA otherwise, same shall be returned back to the government coffers for having been disbursed in violation of government laws, rules and regulations.  The Municipality is also advised to refrain from duplicating the granting of benefit of same nature in future operations.

22.7	We reiterated our recommendation last year that Management should base their PS cap according to Section 325(a) of the Local Government Code in the preparation of the annual budget from which items of PS shall be planned. Stop the practice of appropriating more than what the law for PS limitation requires. Excess expenditures over the PS cap shall be subject for disallowance in conformity with the provisions of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the BAC and the TWG submit full supporting documents to substantiate their claim for honoraria from municipal procurements for the Auditor to render a favorable decision to pass the transaction in audit, otherwise, absence of requirements would force her to disallow them in audit for not conforming with government laws, rules and regulations particularly COA Circular No. 2012-001 and DBM Circular No. 2004-5A.
The Accountant shall adhere to the documentary requirements by advising claimants to submit full supporting documents to their claims for honoraria.

We reiterated that Sections 63 and 68 of PD No. 1445 be implemented to establish proper accountability for collections which could be made anytime whenever audit procedures require. The practice therefore of issuing official receipts immediately for all collections shall be observed. Regarding the collections at the Balete Pass, cash tickets in denominations of P5, P10 and P20 which may be acquired from the National Printing Office could be utilized instead of Accountable Form 51 which  may be issued for greater amounts such as rentals of picnic huts, nipa huts, cottages and the halls. All receipts including unused cash tickets for the day should be accounted with the beginning cash tickets held and the received cash tickets for use to arrive at the total collections which may be remitted daily or as agreed upon for convenience of both the employee and the Municipal Treasurer considering that there are also lean days during the week.
On the sales of the OTOP Store, a regular inventory taking of twice a month may be observed to report items sold from beginning inventory and purchases after an ending inventory is established from physical count. A certain profit percentage on cost should be established per product classification should also be observed to facilitate the computation of sales. Inventory items should be grouped according to nature to facilitate inventory taking and reporting. The Accountable Form No.51 should be issued for the sales at the OTOP Store which should be remitted regularly which can be done once a day. Inventory items shall be encoded in forms which shall be used to account for the quantity at the beginning, quantity received, quantity of inventory end to arrive at the quantity sold.
We also observed that sales of the OTOP store were recorded as  a trust receipt which was rolled over for purchases. Since it is an enterprise in the LGU with grants already received for its proper establishment, the sales of the OTOP Store should be properly accounted as income generated in the General Fund which means that if income increase, then the basis for the limitation of Personnel Services also increase, thus is beneficial for the increase in salaries of the municipal officials and employees
With a new way of handling and managing cash in the economic enterprise, the Municipality is assured of performing better commensurate with the services they render to the people.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) reorganize the Inventory Committee and require the members thereof to
conduct physical count of all properties of the Municipality and submit
the corresponding reports to the Audit Team; and
b) thereafter, instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the resulting
balance of the physical count with the accounting records in order to
present the correct balances of the affected accounts in the financial
statements.
In the event that deficiencies/discrepancies exist between the
Inventory Report and accounting records, the necessary
adjustments/corrections should be made. Also, require the Inventory
Committee to make a report of unserviceable/non-functional PPEs in the
Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) which
could be undertaken simultaneously with the physical inventory for disposal
and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor strictly observe the general limitations on personal services cited under Section 325 (a) of R.A.
No. 7160 and LBC No. 98. Also, she should always be guided by the
conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that
the budgeting requirements are completely complied with. As part of her
fiscal responsibility, she should allow only payments of personal services
costs and other expenditures expressly authorized by law.

In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct the:
a) LDRRM Council to ensure compliance with the provisions of COA
Circular No. 2012-002 in the preparation of the LDRRMFIP.
b) Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere to the accounting and reporting
guidelines set forth under the same Circular. Also, make the necessary
adjusting entries to correctly present the balances of the affected
accounts.
c) LDRRMO and the Municipal Accountant to carefully prepare the
various reports (including the templates) regarding the LDRRMF to be
able to provide accurate and reliable information to the readers thereof.
d) LDRRMO to submit the Certificates of Appearance on the trainings
mentioned in this report to validate his claims on travelling expenses
therefrom.
On the observations noted in the procurement aspect, we
recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC:
a) strictly comply with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No.
9184.
b) judiciously check the documents submitted by the bidders.
c) be cautious in preparing documents to show consistency of information to
the readers of those documents.
d) submit to the Audit Team the copies of POs and their supporting
documents within the period prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-001
to avoid incurrence of delay.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure strict compliance
with Section 96 (a) and (b) of R.A. No. 7160 everytime she will travel outside
the province.

Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant properly
check the claims submitted to the Accounting Office in order to arrive at the
amount of travelling expenses which the itinerant is entitled to.

We recommend that officials responsible for the lapses to submit a
justification and henceforth, ensure that all necessary documents and needed
certifications for the procurement at hand be timely submitted prior to the
issuance of notice of award in adherence to the provisions of R.A. 9184.

We recommend that the members of the Municipal Planning and
Development Council ensure that the projects and programs chargeable
against the 20% development fund are in accordance with DILG and
DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 and to continually update
themselves on the relevant laws and regulations pertaining to the
appropriation and utilization of the special purpose funds.

We recommend that Management speed up the implementation of said
projects and programs funded under the Bottom-Up Budgeting
(BuB) and ensure that funds are optimally utilized with a view of
ensuring efficiency, economy and effectiveness in the utilization of
government resources for the benefit of its constituencies. Otherwise,
Management should return such fund to the source agency so that
appropriate agencies could utilize the same for other equally important
government projects.

We recommend that Management dispose the obsolete and
unserviceable properties pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445 and observe
the mode of disposal in accordance with COA Circular No. 89-296.

We recommend that Management submit justification for the
inordinate proceeding, otherwise responsible persons should be held
accountable for violating the provisions of RA 9184 and its
Implementing Rules and Regulations.

Furthermore, we recommend that Management exert due
diligence in the conduct of its procurement proceedings and ensure that
provisions of the procurement are strictly adhered to avoid personal
liability.

We recommend that the Municipal School Board submit a
justification and likewise submit the MSB’s three-year program for the
Special Education Fund and the School Improvement Plan for evaluation.
Furthermore, we recommend that the Municipal School Board
adhere to the provisions of DEPED DBM DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017 ensuring
the comprehensive, strategic and efficient budget allocation of the SEF.

We recommend that Management require the submission of proof
of identification of the beneficiaries and for applicants other than the
beneficiaries, an appropriate authorization letter with valid ID must
likewise be submitted pursuant to the provisions of PD 1445. Failure of
the Management to adhere shall be a ground for issuance of notice of
disallowance.

We recommend that Management utilize the LCPC Fund in
accordance with the priority programs set forth in DILG Memorandum
Circular No. 2012-120 for the maximum attainment of its purpose.

We recommend that Management submit justification on why the
procurement was awarded to a lone bidder despite having an expired
Philgeps Certificate of Registration Blue Membership and not
disqualified in the post-qualification to avoid disallowance.

We further recommend that the BAC and TWG exercise utmost
prudence in the post-qualification of bids in order to properly determine
whether the bidder concerned complies with and are responsive to all
the requirements and conditions specified in accordance with the R.A.
9184 and its IRR and the GPPB Circular, otherwise, persons responsible
should be held accountable.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant makes the
appropriate adjusting entries and to observe the use of appropriate
account classification in accordance with the Philippine Public Sector
Accounting Standards (PPSASs).

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor issue an executive order
institutionalizing the establishment of the Municipal GAD Focal Point
System Executive Committee in accordance with the structure and
composition provided for in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Planning and Development Officer and the GFPS to lead or spearhead
the setting up and maintenance of a GAD Database to serve as basis for
gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation in
compliance with the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Assessor to process and obtain the Certificate of Title or proof of
absolute ownership over the lots owned by the Municipality located in
Maricaban, Santa Fe.

We further recommend that Management execute a contract or
agreement between these private individuals and the Municipality
outlining the terms and conditions including the rates of rent to be paid
thereof to ensure that the interests of both parties are protected
pursuant to COA Circular No. 88-282A.

We recommended that the constituted Inventory Committee (1) conduct immediately physical inventory taking of all municipal properties, (2) prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment as at December 31, 2017, (3) submit the RPCPPE to the COA as required in consonance with the above-mentioned regulations.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Team conducted inventory taking but was not able to submit the RPCPPE
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No.8, page 51 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (1) consistently prepare the monthly bank reconciliation statements, (2) immediately adjust the books for valid reconciling items, in order to attain the accurate and reliable reporting of the total balance of depository accounts as at the end of the month.
observations.managementAction: Bank reconciliation statements were prepared

observations.recommendation: We recommended that (1) the Municipal officials plan the proper recovery of audit disallowances by applying the provisions of COA Resolution 2017-021 dated November 3, 2017  in the instalment settlement of accounts; (2) prepare cash flow projections from the collection of disallowances per quarter as a target source of additional funds, (3) coordinate with the Disbursing Officer to withhold from the net take home pays of persons liable the agreed semi-monthly/monthly settlements, (4) conduct continuous reminders during municipal assemblies such as Monday flag raising ceremonies on the payments of disallowances, and, (5) see to it that all audit disallowances shall have been collected as at the end of the fiscal year 2018, to protect the best interest of the government and safeguard its scarce resources.
observations.managementAction: Management has been collecting current disallowances.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 18 page 69 of this Report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that at the end of succeeding accounting periods, the Municipality should determine in full all unpaid obligations to be accrued  in the books in order to correctly state the amount of Expenses, Liabilities and Government Equity as at the end of the year and prevent the misstatement of the financial position of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Management has almost paid all its electricity bills, thus, minimal amounts remained.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Liquidating Officer deposit intact their collections daily especially on the last transaction day of every fiscal year in order to safekeep municipal funds at the depository bank and prevent the possibility of loss of scarce resources  through theft and the temptation to misuse funds belonging to the government during the festive season.
observations.managementAction: The Treasurer’s account was increased by an adjustment of staled checks which should be adjusted to Cash-in-Bank.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 10 page 53 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality limit their level of expenditures for Personnel Services in FY 2017 in conformity with the provisions of Section 325 (a) of RA No. 7160 and Section 7 of Joint Resolution of Congress in order to distribute limited municipal resources to various vital purposes with the end in view of attaining optimum delivery of basic services to the constituents.
observations.managementAction: This salary increase was disallowed and is thus being collected from payees.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 11 page 62 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that (1) Management should be discreet in disbursing government funds such that trust receipts should only be used for their intended purposes, (2) refrain from granting cash advances in the Trust Fund unless the nature of the trust receipt requires that it is implemented through the assistance of a disbursing officer, (3) encourage the liquidation/refund of these cash advances to return back to the fund the misused trust receipts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality is liquid enough to meet all its obligations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management (1) comply strictly with the above-cited COA rules and regulations, (2) prepare a semi-monthly schedule of cash advance payments to be withheld per accountable officer which shall be strictly implemented until full payment of accountabilities at the end of 2018. Likewise, (3) enforce full settlement of cash advances to be granted in FY2018 to reflect that the municipality is indeed ready to receive subsidies for development projects.
observations.managementAction: Management has not yet collected old accounts of deceased persons and those separated from the service.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 15 page 62 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management (1) revisit the loan agreements entered into with these persons, (2) the Municipal Agriculturist should initiate the inventory of borrowers and determine their present addresses, (3) establish contact with concerned borrowers and demand collection of their loans in writing, (4) for dead borrowers, collect from their heirs and estate, otherwise, if insolvent, require documents that support their request for write-off of accountabilities which shall be submitted to COA for proper evaluation against the terms and conditions of the agreement and allowable laws and regulations on the subject, (5) enforce immediate settlement for minimal amounts through the assistance of the municipal trial courts on small claims and offer instalments to be paid within the year for the whole amount to be fully paid at the end of the year on larger loan amounts.
observations.managementAction: Management has not yet collected old accounts of deceased persons and those separated from the service.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 14 page 61 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality implement the allotments of the 20% Development Fund in accordance with the prescribed purposes of the JMC which shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures only to enhance the full development of the people of the Municipality of Santa Fe.
observations.managementAction: Management has the same problem of disbursing from the 20%Df those expenditures not aligned to its purpose that it should be paid for expenses which are investment in nature.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 1 page 32 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Development Council identify the projects which are not useful or might give less benefit to the people out of the 20% Development Fund programs, projects and activities  and reprogram the funds in accordance with the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC 2017-1 and implement in full the continuing PPAs in order to support the socio-economic development of the constituency.
observations.managementAction: Same problem still exists in the 20% DF.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 2 page 36 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management attach all relevant supporting documents to transactions to enable the COA to render the proper result of evaluation which is needed by Management in making decisions. The Municipality is then assured that it is paying the commensurate amount of project cost on the actual stage of project completion. Likewise, COA issues either Notice of Suspension or Notice of Disallowance as a result of non-submission of documentary requirements as prescribed in COA Circular 2012-001 and Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Some documents were not fully submitted in the disbursements for the 20%DF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Engineer prepare the annual programs of works of 20% DF programs, projects and activities especially the stages where procurement of materials is required in order to discourage piecemeal shopping purchases of project requirements, instead, procure through competitive bidding to avail of additional income in the form of savings on bulk purchases and the Municipality is also assured of buying items with appropriate quality and specifications.
observations.managementAction: Bidding has been done on all qualified projects of the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC shall be discreet and cautious in the accomplishment of procurement papers especially the documents that indicate the obligations, duties and responsibilities of contractors to protect the best interest of the municipal government, its purposes and its funds.
observations.managementAction: The Notices to proceed were all signed and properly dated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Liquidating Officer (1) report and account all issued accountable forms, (2) refrain from maintaining cash reserves in the municipal coffers,  (3) deposit in full and intact all collections from the issuance of accountable forms in accordance with the pertinent provisions of the Revised Cash Examination Manual in order to safeguard government funds from the risk of possible loss.
observations.managementAction: Minimal amount is not deposited by the Liquidating Officer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that (1) Management should be discreet in granting personnel benefits only to qualified and eligible employees as provided by laws, rules and regulations despite good intentions of giving humane benefits due to economic hardships experienced in the nation, and (2)  Refrain from granting benefits to job orders and contract of services because of the absence of a valid employer-employee relationship.
observations.managementAction: This audit year, Management did not claim CNA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that in the review of every government transaction prior to payment, the Municipal Accountant should attach a checklist of required supporting documents peculiar to the nature of the transaction upon which pre-audit on the completeness of attachments is checked, otherwise, the voucher is returned for want of documents and shall be certified for payment upon full submission of the requirements thereof. The Municipal Treasurer should see to it that all documentary requirements peculiar to each transaction are attached before issuing payment.
observations.managementAction: Management has improved its procedure of requiring sufficient documents to supporting disbursements.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 23 page 82 of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that (1) the Municipal Treasurer stop issuing checks to the Disbursing Officer to fund the payment of RATA in the first week of the month, (2) the Local Chief Executive and the Vice-Mayor stop authorizing early payments of RATA, (3) release RATA benefits only upon rendition of full services by the municipal officials or claims may be prorated based on actual work/attendance and performance, (4) the officials concerned refund in full any excess claim for RATA as a result of incurring deductible absences within the month, in consonance with the above rules and regulations in order to prevent the advance use of municipal money that earns income while held in deposit at the Bank to the best advantage of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: Management complied with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that (1) Municipal officials refrain from reserving the RATA to prioritize personal purposes but their gain shall be the unused remainder after spending for official functions of a month, (2) refrain from deducting from RATA payrolls the repayments for personal loans or any kind of contribution, the RATA shall be free from any encumbrances, (3) officials assigned with government vehicle should stop claiming the Transportation Allowance, and (4) officials using government vehicles should allow prorated deduction from their Transportation Allowance based on the number of days of use in consonance with LBC No. 103 to prevent double charging of the cost of fuel which could be from the TA and at the same time from the payment of fuel to the supplier.
observations.managementAction: Management has complied with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Vice-Mayor limit disbursement of the participants in events representing their respective leagues. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer see to it that the Travel Orders of the officials are within the available funds of their department.
observations.managementAction: Verification of the claims of the travelling expenses of the members of the SB revealed that Travel Orders were already approved by the Municipal Vice-Mayor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to remit to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth the proper withholding and remittance of contributions and loan amortizations intact and within the prescribed period in compliance with R.A. No. 8291, R.A. No. 9679 and R.A. No. 7875 to avoid incurrence of penalties. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant record properly the withholding and remittance of contributions and premiums.
observations.managementAction: During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer observed the proper recording of the withholding contributions and remittance of premiums. Verification showed the withheld contributions and remittance of premiums were remitted to the Philippine Health Insurance Corporation beyond the prescribed period. Furthermore, the Municipality paid interests in CY 2018 totalling P1,357.48 arising from the delay in remittance of contributions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to: a) strictly comply with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; b) judiciously check the documents submitted by the bidders; and c) be cautious in preparing documents to show consistency of information to the readers of those documents.
observations.managementAction: Auditorial review in the procurement of infrastructure projects undertaken by the Municipality revealed substantial compliance with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Results of the procurement undertaken by the Municipality revealed substantial compliance with the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) direct the MPDC to appraise the socio-economic and environmental conditions of the locality and identify the programs and activities to be given priority to address the necessity of the constituents; b) direct the MPDC and the Municipal Engineer to see to it that the projects to be charged against the 20% Development Fund are in line with the allowable development projects enumerated in the DILG and DBM JMC No. 2011-1 amended by JMC No. 2017-1; and c) instruct the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant to include in their reports the continuing projects of the Municipality to be able to provide complete and accurate information to the readers thereof.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor directed the MPDC and the concerned officials and employees to identify priority development programs and to follow the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2011-1 as amended. Some audit recommendations were implemented.
observations.reason: Some observations were already addressed in CY 2018. Reiterated in Finding No. 6 of this report. The BAC implemented some audit recommendations; however some procurement activities for goods in CY 2018 still disclosed lapses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) direct the GFPS to carefully prepare the GAD Plan and Budget and to fully address the gender issues and concerns of the organization; b) ensure the GFPS coordinate with the Municipal Accountant in the preparation of the GAD AR; and c) comply strictly with the guidelines in the preparation of the LGU GAD AR provided in the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 amended by JMC No. 2016-01.
observations.managementAction: The GFPS already submitted the Accomplishment Reports to the Audit Team for CY 2017 and submitted actions taken by the LGU on the COA audit findings and recommendations in CY 2017.
observations.reason: Other observations in the previous year were also addressed in CY 2018 audit. Reiterated in Finding No. 7 of this report. The BAC implemented some of the audit recommendations but procurement lapses remained for CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management stop the practice of recognizing fund transfers received for specific purpose as regular income to avoid incurring excess of PS cost over PS limitation. The Municipal Mayor should observe the general limitations on personal services under Section 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98 dated October 14, 2011 and be guided by the conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets. As part of her fiscal responsibility, she should allow payments of personal services costs and other expenditures only expressly authorized by law.
observations.managementAction: During the year, the Municipal Management stopped the practice of recognizing specific purpose fund transfers as regular income to avoid incurring excess of PS cost over PS limitation.
observations.reason: For CY 2018, the Municipality still exceeded the PS limitation by P5,613,418.41 per budget and P3,431,480.90 per actual expenditure. Reiterated in Finding No. 2 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management exert effort in determining if the National Solid Waste Management Commission approved their 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan and to comply strictly with the provisions of R.A. No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2017, the Municipal Solid Waste Management Coordinator approved their 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan. Moreover, the Municipality prepared Safe Closure and Rehabilitation Plan which was submitted to DENR-EMB. The Municipality implemented the project - Establishment and Operation of Material Recovery Facility funded by DENR-EMB.
observations.reason: The 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan (2013-2022) submitted by the Municipality was conditionally approved by the NSWMC. The purpose of the Plan is the closure of the open dumpsite. The target date of the Municipality towards the establishment of sanitary landfill is by year 2023. Reiterated in Finding No. 12 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a) exert effort to transfer the unexpended balance of previous years into the Special Trust Fund and to strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and b) prepare the LDRRMFIP that supports disaster risk management activities and make the necessary adjustments in the accounting records in accordance with the same Circular.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: In CY 2017, the Municipal Accountant had already transferred the unexpended balance of previous years to the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF for CY 2016. However, the unexpended balance of 30% QRF and DRRM-MOOE for previous years were still not presented under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP for the CY 2018. Reiterated in Finding No. 3 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to strictly comply with the requirements of the 2009 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, judiciously check the documents submitted by the bidders and be cautious in preparing documents to show consistency of information to the readers of those documents.
observations.managementAction: The BAC implemented some of the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Results of the auditorial review of the procurement of goods for CY 2018 still disclosed lapses in the procurement activities undertaken by the BAC.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to strictly comply with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, judiciously check the documents submitted by the bidders and be cautious in preparing documents to show consistency of information to the readers of those documents.
observations.managementAction: The BAC implemented some of the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Results of the auditorial review of procurement of goods for CY 2018 still disclosed lapses in the procurement activities undertaken by the BAC.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, should see to it that all the necessary documentary requirements relative to disbursements for infrastructure projects including adherence to the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 be strictly implemented.
observations.managementAction: Some of the documentary requirements were submitted to the Audit Team and compliance to pertinent rules and regulations on disbursements for infrastructure projects were observed.
observations.reason: Some of the prior years' lacking documentary requirements were already submitted and addressed. Reiterated in Finding No. 5 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a) the Municipal Mayor along with all concerned officials and employees identify priority development projects that constitute capital expenditures and strictly follow DILG-DBM JMC No. 2011-1; b) instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Council to identify priority development projects and refrain from incorporating lump sum or generic term appropriations; c) direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain a separate account as prescribed by Section 313 (c) of R.A. No. 7160 and necessary subsidiary ledgers; and d) prepare a monitoring report specifying the programs planned vis-à-vis accomplishments.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor issued instructions to the concerned officials and employees to identify priority development projects and to follow DILG-DBM JMC No. 2011-1.
observations.reason: Some of the observations were addressed in CY 2018 but lapses remained. Reiterated in Finding No. 6 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a) the Local Chief Executive ensure adequate allocation of at least five percent (5%) of the total budget for GAD for priority programs, projects and activities that address gender issues and concerns; and b) the GAD Focal Point System members ensure that PPAs incorporated in the GAD Plan constitute gender issues and address women's concerns and that GAD Planning and Budgeting training be conducted.
observations.managementAction: The appropriations appearing in the GAD Plan for CY 2018 satisfied the minimum allocation of at least five percent for GAD of the total budget. The GFPS members administered the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) test during the planning and implementation phases. The GFPS submitted to the Audit Team actions taken by the LGU on the COA audit findings and recommendations in CY 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: a) prepare and submit Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements for all the LGU’s bank accounts together with the paid checks, debit/credit memos for verification as provided for under Section 74 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-011. Also make an extra effort to submit the bank reconciliation statements on time.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant was advised to give priority to the timely submission of the accounts and financial reports within the reglementary period as required under COA Circulars. During CY 2018, Bank reconciliation statements under the Special Education Fund for the months of April 2014 to July 2014 were lacking. The Audit Team observed significant delay in the submission of monthly bank reconciliation statements for CY 2018.
observations.reason: Verification revealed delay was still incurred by the Accounting Office in the submission of the accounts and monthly trial balances to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a) instruct all collectors to prepare RCDs and remit all collections to the Municipal Treasurer in accordance with Section 29, Vol. 1 of the NGAS Manual; b) be more circumspect in the acknowledgment of remittances in terms of accuracy of the amount; c) refrain from allowing incomplete remittances from the collecting officers; and d) Municipal Accountant ensure the accuracy of the amounts of recorded transactions and adjust the balance of the accounts affected by the miscalculated collections and refrain from charging the unremitted collections to receivable account.
observations.managementAction: Collections during the year were remitted to the Municipal Treasurer. Verification of the Report of Collections and Deposits for CY 2018 disclosed that the daily report includes collections on prior periods. The Municipal Accountant adjusted recorded transactions as needed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to observe strictly the provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 specifically in the procurement of infrastructure projects to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring and to properly address the above noted lapses.
observations.managementAction: The BAC implemented some of the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Lapses were still noted in the review of procurement activities of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to observe strictly the provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 specifically in the procurement of infrastructure projects to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring and to properly address the above noted lapses.
observations.managementAction: The BAC implemented some of the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Lapses were still noted in the review of procurement activities of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, should see to it that all the necessary documentary requirements attached to the vouchers are accurate and complete before processing the claims.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken by Management.
observations.reason: Some of the necessary documentary requirements were already submitted. Reiterated in Finding No. 5 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended: 1. The Municipal Mayor ensure adequate allocation of at least five percent (5%) of the total budget for GAD for priority programs, projects and activities that address gender issues and concerns; and 2. The GAD Focal Point System members undergo gender sensitivity training and GAD Planning and Budgeting to address the findings and ensure proper monitoring and implementation.
observations.managementAction: The appropriations appearing in the GAD Plan for CY 2018 satisfied the minimum allocation of at least five percent for GAD of the total budget. The GFPS members administered the HGDG test during the planning and implementation phases and submitted responses to the COA findings.

Starting Jan. 1, 2018, all pertinent documentary requirements relative to all procurement have been completely attached not just a mere certification of the BAC Secretariat that documents are on file.

AAR Since the management received this AOM, the BAC already included Philippine Chamber of Constructors Association to be sent with notices to abide with the minimum required number of observers.
Appropriate proof of receipt were also attached to DVs upon payment as part of the documentary requirement.

AAR Copies of MOA of CY BuB Projects having one, were provided to COA Auditors after exit conference. As sometime in March 2017, a written request was sent to DBM Regional Office to ask copies of SARO of prior years, to no avail. As of to date, most of BuB projects have been implemented and liquidated already.

AAR Since the CY 2017 Physical Inventory Report for 2017 was submitted. As of October 31, 2018 new list has been prepared with the corrected AAR figures and new CY additions for all acquired assets from January to date.
AAR Posting of Performance Security within 2017 are now within the reglementary period as recommended.

AAR Since the delay was attributable to the system of obtaining resolution authorizing LCE to enter into contracts and not from the end of the lone bidder or suppliers, the management had done alternative measures not to violate the prescribed period in entering into contracts with winning bidders in accordance with the provisions of RA 9184 and the IRR in the procurement process.

AAR Management has been more careful in the utilization of 20% Development Fund to conform with DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1. Liquidated damages recommended for collection from Heroes Technology has been done last February 27, 2018 of which Official Receipt has been attached to reply to AOM.

AAR Below please find 2017 allocation and distribution on the proceeds of Tourism Ecological Fee for CY 2017 pursuant to the amended Municipal Ordinance No. 28-2017 as follows:
Tourism – P293,000.00
Environmental – P462,560.00
Ecological Fee amounting to P30.00/ tourist presently distributed/allocated as follows:
* P6.00 share or 20% of gross collection shall accrue to the Tourism Office and shall be used for administrative expenses such as printing of tickets, salaries of personnel, production of IEC materials, office supplies, tourism brochures and establishment of tourism office;
*P9.00 or 30% of gross collection shall be used and spent only for environmental and tourism projects such as, but not limited to: 1) rehabilitation of natural resources such as marine and upland resources; 2) effective environmental law enforcement (includes expenditures such as Bantay Dagat activities, fuel and maintenance of patrol boats and other similar activities that will help enhance and improve municipality’s ecosystem); 3) Solid Waste Management; 4) Tourism and other environmental related programs
*The remaining P15.00 or 50% of gross collection shall accrue to the Local Treasury that will form part of the General Fund
AAR Several Trainings on RA 9184 or the Government Procurement Reform Act have already been attended by BAC Members in Cebu and in Manila.

AAR Mr. Levi Espinosa agreed to pay P16,000.00 the Disallowance soonest possible time. He plans to apply for monetization of leave credits to cover his liability and get cleared from the disallowed benefit.

AAR The management has implemented the minimum wage rate of P308/day. To those JO personnel who have not been adjusted of their rates, their working hours have been shortened to conform with the minimum standards set forth by government regulating bodies.
AAR Hiring of Job Order personnel is in effect to fill in departments that are undermanned. They are tasked to do clerical, administrative and field works in a case to case basis.
AAR Execution of the Annual Contract of Lease were done starting January 2018 between LGU and the lessees legally notarized by a Notary Public to ensure protection of both parties.

AAR The Solid Waste Management Plan has already been approved last October 29, 2018. All of the 10 barangays have established Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) though four of them are not yet functional. As of this report, 2 out of 10 MRFs are not yet functional.

AAR All disbursements for traveling expenses charged against LCPC appropriation were all reasonable and pertinent to the implementation of our LCPC plans. All disbursements charged to the said fund were believed to be in accordance with the standard guidelines set forth in line with our LGU advocacy to protect the rights of children.

AAR The Sangguniang Bayan has issued Resolution No. 69-2018 dated March 9, 2018 respectfully requesting COA to write off said receivable. Request was then submitted but was returned for farther compliance of supporting documents pursuant to COA Circular 2016-005. Available documents regarding grant have already been submitted to COA last January 31, 2019 for their perusal of our request.

AAR The office has now a separate special account in the General Ledger as required under Section 313 of Republic Act 7160 and as prescribed pursuant to policies related on this per NGAS Manual of LGUs. Complete set of financial reports has been finally established October 2018.

AAR Municipal Accountant is waiting for the submission of reports on sales, collections and disbursements from 2012 to 2017 from the Treasury Office on the operation of Fish Drying Facility. Once reports are rendered by Treasury Office, corresponding subsidiary ledgers will be established immediately to obtain figures for Financial Reports generation. Ms. Cristina Gilbuena is the one tasked to do all the recordings by the Municipal Treasurer and is still on-going as of the moment.
AAR All terminated/separated/retired employees have been deducted of disallowed benefit from their respective Terminal Leave Pays.
AAR All terminated/separated/retired employees have been deducted of disallowed benefit from their respective Terminal Leave Pays.
AAR Collections on the last working day of December 2018 must be deposited on the next banking day of 2019. Being the longest day of the year, huge cash in vault balance is expected.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Accountable Officer:
Restitute the amount of cash shortage;
Stop the irregular practices in handling of cash; and
Comply with the aforementioned provisions of P.D. No. 1445.
observations.managementAction: Disbursing officer incurred shortage while Liquidating Officer incurred overage.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that management prepare and submit its Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) within the period prescribed by the law. Likewise, submit the AAPSI of CY 2015 audit findings and recommendations as soon as possible using the prescribed form under COA Memorandum No. 2014 – 002 dated March 18, 2014. It is further recommended that a focal person be designated by the Local Chief Executive who will consolidate the report before submission to the audit team.
observations.managementAction: Management failed to comply with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management monitor and disburse Personnel Services within its limitation or cap in order not to avert funding for other plans, projects and programs   in the annual budget. Also, we further recommend that management disburse funds within its approved appropriation to avoid overdraft. Likewise, salaries and benefits of public health workers should conform to the rate that corresponds with the class of the municipality and should be adjusted accordingly
observations.managementAction: Management incurred another excess in the PS Expenditures.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management through the Bids and Awards Committee and the Municipal Accounting Office ensure that the contract and all its supporting documents including those required in auditorial review be submitted to the Audit Team within five (5) days from its execution to facilitate its review and evaluation as required in COA Circulars No. 96-010 and 2009-001.
observations.managementAction: Management failed to comply with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management: 

Require the collectors to remit daily all their collections to the Municipal Treasurer who in turn will deposit such to authorize government bank regularly; and
 
Comply with the rules and regulations on collections and deposits as mandated by Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 32 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: Huge amount of undeposited collections could be found in the Fs as at year-end.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that accountable officers avoid using government monies for personal transactions and that collections be safeguarded from possible misuse or misappropriation to avoid possible filing of administrative or criminal action against them.
observations.managementAction: The Liquidating Officer incurred cash overage while the Disbursing Officer incurred cash shortage
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 21, Page 69, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management adhere to the provisions of R.A. No. 9003 governing the operation and management of a sanitary landfill for the protection of public health and the environment and that ownership of the present site of the sanitary landfill be transferred to the municipality either through purchase or donation to avoid conflict in the future.
observations.managementAction: The municipality still operates a controlled dumpsite.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 30, Page 84, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that management prepare and submit its Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation (AAPSI) within the period prescribed by the law. Likewise, submit the AAPSI of CY 2014 audit findings and recommendations as soon as possible.
observations.managementAction: The municipality prepared and submitted its Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation for CY 2014.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the officials responsible in processing payrolls that its vouchers be supported with complete documentary requirements in adherence to the above cited provisions to ensure the validity of the claims.
observations.managementAction: Disbursement vouchers were still not supported with complete documentary requirements.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 24, Page 74, Part II of the report

observations.managementAction: Management did not deposit its collection at year – end.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 10, Page 51, Part II of the report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management monitor and disburse Personnel Services within its limitation or cap in order not to avert funding for other plans, projects and programs   in the annual budget. Also, we further recommended that management disburse funds within its approved appropriation to avoid overdraft.
observations.managementAction: Management monitored its disbursements on Personnel Services but still exceeded on PS limitation.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 9, Page 48, Part II of the report

observations.managementAction: The management complied with said recommendation but there were still unliquidated cash advances since most accountable officers were not in the service anymore.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 13, Page 56, Part II of the report

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive enforce settlement of these disallowances in compliance to COA Circular No. 2009-006.
observations.managementAction: The Local Chief Executive enforced payment for said disallowances but most persons liable are not in the service anymore.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 5, Page 41, Part II of the report

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to exert extra efforts to identify the breakdown of P254,194.30 balance from 2007.  Send demand letters to identified accountable officers and pursue the liquidation or settlement thereof through refund and/or withholding of salaries.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason: The balance amounting P254,194.30 pertaining to prior years’ cash advances remained unliquidated as of December 31, 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the lacking documents aforementioned be submitted to the Audit Team and compliance to pertinent rules and regulations relative to disbursements for infrastructure projects including the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 be strictly implemented.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.

We recommended that:

the Municipal Mayor along with all the concerned officials and employees identify the priority needs of its constituents and strictly follow the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 of the DILG and DBM as updated under Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01 dated February 22, 2017 in the implementation of projects/
programs/
activities for the 20% Development Fund;

the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Council to identify priority development projects that constitute the nature of capital expenditures and refrain from incorporating lump sum or generic term appropriations to avoid insertions of projects, programs and activities that are not related to the implementation of the 20% Development Fund;

Management should prepare a monitoring report specifying the programs as planned vis-à-vis accomplishments in order to keep track of the unimplemented projects during the year.
observations.managementAction: No action taken by Management.
observations.reason:
Same observations were noted in CY 2017 audit.
